macro_segments.title: Review of Previous Discussion: The Strength and Scope of Ma'amar According to Beit Shammai

micro_segments.title: Opening: Correction of Yesterday's Explanation and Reframing the Core Question
The Mishnah Case: Two Sisters, Sequential Deaths, and Ma'amar with Rachel
Beit Shammai's Position: Ma'amar Frees Leah as an Ervah
Rabbi Elazar's Initial Claim: Ma'amar Is Not Full Acquisition but Pushes Off the Tzarah
Gemara's Test: Can Ma'amar Solve the Achot Zekukato Problem in a Simultaneous Death Case?
Key Principle: Ma'amar DeHeteira Works, Ma'amar De'Issura Does Not
Rav Ashi's Alternative Reading: Ma'amar Only Partially Pushes Off, Leaving Some Connection to Leah
Gemara's Rejection of Rav Ashi: The Mishnah Clearly States Leah Walks Away
The Yerushalmi's Source: Deriving Beit Shammai's Ma'amar from Ve-Lakachah Lo Le-Ishah

macro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Does Ma'amar Create Kidusin, Full Acquisition, or Even Nisuin?

micro_segments.title: The Question Posed: Whether Ma'amar Constitutes Nisuin with All Its Consequences
Abaye's Response: What Would Nisuin Add Beyond Kidusin?
The Distinction Between Arusah and Nesuah: Inheritance, Tumah, and Annulment of Vows
Rava's Reasoning: If Standard Kidusin Without Nisuin Lacks These Rights, Why Assume Ma'amar Differs?
The Alternative Phrasing: Does Ma'amar Require a Separate Act of Chuppah (Mesirah LeChuppah)?
The Sex Act in Yibum: Does Consensual Yibum Itself Constitute Chuppah and Nisuin?
The Problem of Non-Consensual Yibum: How Yibum Can Work Even Without Agreement
Rabba's Resolution: Ma'amar Removes the Zikas Yavamin, Shifting Entirely to the Kidusin Track

macro_segments.title: Tannaitic Sources on Yibamah and Hafarat Nedarim: Connecting Ma'amar to Marital Status

micro_segments.title: Bringing in the Mishnah: A Case of Shomer Yavam with Multiple Brothers
Rabbi Eliezer, Rabbi Yehoshua, and Rabbi Akiva on Vow Annulment for a Yibamah
The Gemara's Question: Why Can Rabbi Eliezer Annul Vows if the Relationship Is Only Partial?
Rav Ami's Solution: The Mishna Assumes Ma'amar Was Already Performed, Creating Full Status
Rav Nachman's Clarification: Even If Ma'amar Makes Arusah Status, Hafarat Nedarim Requires the Father
Alternative Explanation: The Wife Does It With an Implicit Stipulation Based on Court-Ordered Support

macro_segments.title: Three Brothers Married to Two Sisters and an Unrelated Woman: Sequential Deaths and Forbidden Statuses

micro_segments.title: Case 1: Reuven (married to Rachel) dies, Levi yibums with Leah, then Levi dies—Rachel's forbidden status
Sub-case 1A: If Levi Did Ma'amar Instead of Yibum—Rachel Is Freed, Dvorah Remains Problematic
Gemara's Inference: No Zika When the First Brother Dies Without Yibum—Rachel Should Be Freed to Shimon
Case 2: Levi (married to Dvorah) dies, Reuven yibums with Dvorah and Rachel, then Reuven dies—parallel structure
Sub-case 2A: If Reuven Did Ma'amar with Dvorah Instead of Yibum—same forbidden outcome
Gemara's Question: Why Teach Two Cases When the Second Is Obvious from the First (Kal Va-Chomer)?
Resolution: The First Mishnah Was a Later Chiddush; The Author Kept Both for Pedagogical Reasons

macro_segments.title: The Principle of Forbidden-at-Moment-of-Death: Achot Ishto and Eshet Achiv Status

micro_segments.title: Case 3: Reuven Dies (Rachel Cannot Do Yibum with Shimon), Later Leah Dies—Rachel Still Forbidden
Rav's Rule: A Yibamah Not Named at the Moment of Death Becomes Eshet Achiv and Remains Forbidden
Why Case 3 Differs: When Only One Brother Can Act, Status Changes End the Process
But When Multiple Brothers Remain: Status Change During the Waiting Period Should Not Affect Final Status
The Dual-Status Model: Rachel Is Both Achot Ishto and Eshet Achiv, the Latter Persisting

macro_segments.title: Case 4: Divorce Instead of Death—Tzarah Becomes Permitted When Original Wife Is Divorced

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Setup: Shimon Divorces Leah, Levi Dies, Shimon Yibums with Dvorah
Leah's Status After Divorce: She Is No Longer Part of the Yibum Equation
Dvorah's Status: She Can Now Be Yibummed by Shimon Because Leah's Divorce Removes the Achot Ishto Barrier
Final Teaching: When All Brothers Die or Wives Are Divorced/Dead, Tzarot Are Permitted
